food_subgroup: Paneer cubes in rich tomato-cashew-butter sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Paneer Butter Masala. Nearest parent is a dairy-based curry. A new concept covering the popular 'makhani' sauce family is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Dairy-based dish (paneer) in tomato-butter sauce, North Indian style — closest parent match within FoodEx2 composite dish hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Paneer Butter Masala. A03SM (composite dairy dish) is the closest defensible parent. Exact code pending EFSA extension for South Asian composite dishes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Paneer Butter Masala as of April 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Paneer Butter Masala is approximately 200 g of curry (without rice or bread), providing around 424 kcal, 14.4 g protein, 33 g fat, and 19 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer Butter Masala — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry F01040002
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN entry F01040002 for Paneer Butter Masala. Trace minerals copper, manganese, and vitamin K are supplemented from enriched_json cross-reference at confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI from analogous high-fat, high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate tomato-based curry — University of Sydney GI Database reference class
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Paneer Butter Masala in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 28 is estimated from the closest analogous food class: high-fat, high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate tomato-based curry dishes. The high fat content (16.5 g/100g) and protein content (7.2 g/100g) significantly slow gastric emptying and carbohydrate digestion, supporting a low GI estimate. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (28 × 9.5 × 2) / 100 = 5.32, rounded to 5.3. This is consistent with the input record's referenced estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (358 mg/100g; ~716 mg per 200 g serving) from butter, cream, and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Paneer Butter Masala to a half portion (≤100 g) and request reduced-salt, reduced-butter preparations; home-cooked versions allow significant sodium reduction, but restaurant servings typically exceed 600 mg sodium per bowl and should be consumed only occasionally.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~28) and low glycemic load (~5.3 per 200 g serving) due to high fat and protein content slowing carbohydrate digestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer Butter Masala has a low GI (~28) and a glycemic load of approximately 5 per standard serving, making it suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; however, the high saturated fat content (9.2 g/100g) warrants attention to overall dietary fat intake and cardiovascular risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (9.2 g/100g) and cholesterol (52 mg/100g) from butter, cream, and paneer, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Paneer Butter Masala sparingly due to its high saturated fat (9.2 g/100g), cholesterol (52 mg/100g), and sodium (358 mg/100g); a 200 g serving provides approximately 18.4 g saturated fat, which exceeds the recommended daily limit of 20 g for most adults, and should be reserved as an occasional indulgence with portion control.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat from butter and cream may promote systemic inflammation if consumed in excess
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should moderate intake of Paneer Butter Masala due to its high saturated fat content; tomato lycopene and kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves) provide modest anti-inflammatory compounds, but these do not offset the pro-inflammatory potential of excess saturated fat at restaurant serving sizes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Butter Masala, also known as Paneer Makhani, is one of North India's most celebrated and widely recognised vegetarian curries. It consists of soft, fresh cubes of paneer (Indian cottage cheese) simmered in a luxuriously smooth, mildly spiced, orange-red sauce made from puréed tomatoes, fried onion, cashew nut paste, generous amounts of butter (makhan), and fresh cream. The sauce is always blended until completely smooth, producing a distinctly silky, velvety texture that is characteristic of the dish. A signature aromatic note comes from kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves) added at the end of cooking. The dish is mildly spiced with cinnamon, cardamom, and garam masala, making it approachable for those who prefer less heat. Nutritionally, it is rich in calcium (172 mg/100g) and vitamin A (128 mcg RAE/100g) from the paneer and dairy components, and provides moderate protein (7.2 g/100g). However, it is high in saturated fat (9.2 g/100g) and sodium (358 mg/100g), making it an indulgence dish best consumed in moderation. Its low glycemic index (~28) and low glycemic load (~5.3 per 200 g serving) make it relatively suitable for people managing blood glucose, though the high saturated fat content remains a concern for cardiovascular health. It is a staple in Indian restaurants worldwide and is best served with naan, kulcha, or jeera rice.
common_user_queries: paneer butter masala vs palak paneer

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN entry F01040002, which is the primary approved source for Indian dishes. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are complete. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database reference class, clearly documented. Ontology uses a closest-parent FoodEx2 code with an honest gap note. All mandatory multilingual fields are populated.
notes: Three trace nutrients (copper 0.12 mg, manganese 0.08 mg, vitamin K 2.5 mcg) are sourced from enriched_json cross-reference at confidence 0.9 rather than directly from ICMR-NIN; these are plausible for this food matrix and retained. GI value of 28 is an analogous estimate — no direct measurement for Paneer Butter Masala exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Portions in the input contained multiple duplicate entries and multiple is_default:true flags; the canonical serving size of 200 g (curry only) has been selected as the default for glycemic load calculation. The input source URL (https://www.nin.res.in/) is the ICMR-NIN homepage; a direct deep-link to the specific entry is not publicly available. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported by ICMR-NIN for this entry and are set to -1.
